agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands no location sharing due to driver retention problems. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that cannot be resolved by technical design alone — it requires executive alignment on business strategy.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver employed or contracted by the company to transport packages from origin to destination."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The current GPS coordinates of a courier, updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to deliver a specific package to a customer."},{"term":"Customer visibility","definition":"The degree to which delivery tracking information is exposed to the end customer."},{"term":"Driver retention","definition":"The company's ability to keep couriers employed/contracted over time — a critical operational metric in logistics."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The level of precision at which a courier's position is shared — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to approximate zone or ETA-only."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary at a personal stop (restroom, fuel) during delivery — exact GPS exposes private behavior","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until contradiction is resolved. This scenario highlights why granularity and privacy policy must be decided at the business level first."},{"scenario":"Courier is in a dangerous neighborhood and customer shares location with a third party","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until contradiction is resolved. This scenario highlights courier safety risks of full transparency."},{"scenario":"Courier is multi-dropping (carrying multiple customers' deliveries) — location reveals other customers' approximate addresses","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. Cross-customer privacy implications require policy decision."},{"scenario":"Customer calls support demanding to know why courier appears stationary","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. Depends on what visibility level is granted."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High impact in both directions. Customer Success argues transparency drives acquisition and retention (revenue). Operations argues driver churn costs $3K-$8K per driver replacement in logistics (recruiting, onboarding, lost capacity). Both are valid financial concerns — the question is which cost dominates, and that requires data neither team has presented here.","operational":"Showing exact GPS creates support volume (customers calling about stationary couriers), driver dissatisfaction, and potential safety liability. NOT showing location sacrifices a feature that is increasingly table-stakes in last-mile delivery (Uber, DoorDash, Amazon all offer it in some form). The operational impact depends entirely on the resolution path chosen.","user":"Customers expect some form of delivery visibility — the industry norm is a map with an approximate indicator plus ETA. Couriers expect reasonable privacy protections — the industry norm is that exact coordinates are NOT shared, but an abstracted representation is. Both user groups have legitimate expectations.","risk":"CRITICAL RISK: Building to either team's spec without resolving the contradiction guarantees organizational conflict, rework, and stakeholder trust erosion. Shipping exact GPS without driver consent may violate labor privacy regulations in some jurisdictions (GDPR, state labor laws). Shipping no visibility at all may put the company at a competitive disadvantage.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Desire delivery visibility for peace of mind, planning, and trust. Exact GPS is not necessarily what they need — ETA and proximity may satisfy the core job-to-be-done."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected by surveillance concerns, safety risks, and feeling of autonomy. Driver retention is existentially important to a logistics company — no drivers means no deliveries."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Owns customer satisfaction metrics. Their requirement reflects customer feedback but may overindex on 'exact GPS' when the underlying need is 'delivery predictability.'"},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Owns driver retention and fleet capacity. Their blanket prohibition may overindex on the problem — some drivers may not object to abstracted tracking."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED. Must weigh in on labor privacy, data protection, and consent requirements before any location-sharing feature is built."},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. This is a strategic trade-off between customer experience and workforce retention — a business decision, not a technical one."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the specification contains a direct, unresolved contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder teams. Both requirements are presented as non-negotiable, which means the spec as written is unbuildable. No acceptance criteria can be defined until executive leadership resolves the strategic trade-off. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not been consulted, which could invalidate either position. The 35 reflects that we cannot even define what 'correct' means yet.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and material — not a misunderstanding that can be resolved by rewording","Both stakeholder concerns are legitimate and grounded in real business dynamics","The logistics industry has established patterns (approximate location + ETA) that could inform a compromise","Legal/compliance review is mandatory before any location-sharing feature"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which business priority should take precedence (customer transparency vs driver retention)","Whether a middle-ground solution (approximate location, ETA-only, opt-in) would satisfy both teams","Regulatory requirements for courier location sharing in the company's operating jurisdictions","Whether the driver retention problem is actually caused by location tracking or by other factors"],"assumptions":["Both teams are accurately representing their stakeholders' concerns","No executive decision has already been made that supersedes these requirements","The company operates in jurisdictions where employee/contractor location privacy is a legal consideration"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers at all times during delivery. Operations requires NEVER showing courier location to customers. These are mutually exclusive requirements from two teams with equal authority over their domains. This cannot be resolved at the implementation level — it is a strategic business decision about the trade-off between customer transparency and driver retention/privacy.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers/Drivers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Legal/Compliance","Executive leadership"],"options":["A: Full GPS transparency (Customer Success wins, Operations loses — risk driver attrition)","B: No location sharing (Operations wins, Customer Success loses — risk competitive disadvantage)","C: Compromise — approximate location (map zone or street-level, not exact GPS) with ETA, shown only during active delivery window. This is the industry standard pattern.","D: Opt-in model — couriers consent to approximate location sharing; customers see ETA-only for non-consenting couriers","E: Defer to Legal/Compliance first — determine what is even permissible before choosing a product direction"],"recommendation":"ESCALATE TO EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP. Recommend Option E first (legal review), then Option C or D as the likely resolution. The industry-standard pattern is approximate location + ETA, which addresses the customer's core job-to-be-done (predictability) without exposing exact GPS (driver privacy). Exact GPS coordinates serve no customer need that approximate location + accurate ETA does not also serve. However, this is a business strategy decision that requires executive arbitration — I will not define acceptance criteria against a contradictory spec."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Contradictory requirements: 'must show exact GPS at all times' vs 'must never show location'","business_rule_broken":"No coherent business rule can be derived from mutually exclusive stakeholder requirements. The spec is unbuildable as written."},{"violation":"Legal/Compliance stakeholder not consulted","business_rule_broken":"Any feature involving sharing employee/contractor real-time location with third parties must pass legal review before requirements are finalized. This is a governance prerequisite, not an optional step."}]}
